DEMURRER to 1ST Amended COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Apr-05-2021 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
DEFENDANT B-SIDE, INC., DBA "B-SIDE CONSTRUCTION", ANTON KALAFATI's DEMURRER to 1ST Amended COMPLAINT.

No appearances. The tentative ruling is adopted as follows:

Defendant's demurrer to the first cause of action is sustained as unopposed; Defendant's demurrer to the second cause of action for negligence is sustained without leave to amend; and Defendant's demurrers to the First Amended Complaint in its entirety and to the fifth cause of action are overruled.

Defendant's demurrer to the entire complaint under Bus. & Prof. Code section 7031 is overruled. "[T]his chapter does not apply to any person who engages in the activities herein regulated as an employee who receives wages as his or her sole compensation, does not customarily engage in an independently established business, and does not have the right to control or discretion as to the manner of performance so as to determine the final results of the work performed." (Bus. & Prof. Code sec. 7053.) Defendant does not show from the face of the complaint that Plaintiff is not an employee. "Respondents in the present case were employees who did not act like unlicensed independent contractors. Business and Professions Code section 7053 explains that the prohibition of Business and Professions Code section 7031 does not apply to a person who receives wages as his sole compensation, who does not engage in an independent business, and who cannot control how the work is performed." (Sanders Construction Co., Inc. v. Cerda (2009) 175 Cal.App.4th 430, 436.) While the court may take judicial notice of contradictory allegations in earlier pleadings under the sham pleading doctrine, Plaintiff's allegation in the original complaint that he "was a sub-contractor on several job sites, working under direction and control of Defendants," is sufficiently ambiguous that it does not preclude him from alleging that he was Defendants' employee. (See Herman v. Los Angeles County Metropolitan Transportation Authority (1999) 71 Cal.App.4th 819, 823, fn. 5 ["While factual allegations in a petition that are contradicted by petitioner's statements in other related documents will be rejected . . . , that is not the case here."].) Discovery will determine whether Plaintiff was an employee or an unlicensed subcontractor. (See, e.g., WSS Industrial Construction, Inc. v. Great West Contractors, Inc. (2008) 162 Cal.App.4th 581 [trial court erred in denying nonsuit in action by unlicensed subcontractor against general contractor to recover for work performed under construction services agreement].)

Defendant's demurrer to the second cause of action for negligence is sustained without leave to amend. The economic loss rule bars recovery in tort for Plaintiff's contract-based claims.

Defendant's demurrer to the fifth cause of action for misrepresentation is overruled. Plaintiff's allegations that Defendant entered into agreements that were "partly written" and "partly verbal," made "verbal promises to pay Plaintiff and to reimburse Plaintiff," and that those representations were made "from mid-2016 through May 2017," are sufficient. (See FAC, pars. 18, 43.)

Order signed.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Jacqueline Alameda; Not Reported. (302/EPS)

Notice And Motion To Set Aside Default
Off Calendar Feb-17-2021 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANTS B-SIDE, INC., DBA "B-SIDE CONSTRUCTION", ANTON KALAFATI'S Motion To Set Aside Default:

Defendants B Side, Inc. and Anton Kalfati's motion to set aside the defaults entered December 29, 2020 is granted. Under Code of Civil Procedure section 473(b), a "court may, upon any terms as may be just, relieve a party or his or her legal representative from a judgment, dismissal, order, or other proceeding taken against him or her through his or her mistake, inadvertence, surprise, or excusable neglect." "It is the policy of the law to favor, whenever possible, a hearing on the merits Therefore, when a party in default moves promptly to seek relief, very slight evidence is required to justify a trial court's order setting aside a default." (Shamblin v. Brattain (1988) 44 Cal.3d 474, 478.) Here, Defendants show that the defaults were entered against them after they had met and conferred with plaintiff regarding his first amended complaint and he had represented that he intended to file a further amended complaint, but he thereafter failed to do so and ignored defendants' numerous communications. (Bowne Decl. pars. 6-11.) Moreover, although plaintiff is representing himself in propria persona, he should have warned defendants' counsel before taking their defaults. (Cf. LaSalle v. Vogel (2019) 36 Cal.App.5th 127, 135 ["it is now well acknowledged that an attorney has an ethical obligation to warn opposing counsel that the attorney is about to take an adversary's default"].)

order is signed.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman, Clerk: S. Le, Not Reported. =(302/EPS)
